hypotheses: Individuals under mortality salience will be less likely to give utilitarian responses to moral conflicts.
Mortality salience will have no comparable effect on control scenarios where utilitarian and intuitive responses are congruent.
The detrimental effect of mortality salience on utilitarian conflict judgments will be comparable to that of an extreme concurrent cognitive load.
claim.hypotheses_location: Section 4, 'The current research' (paragraph stating predictions about mortality salience effects and control scenarios).
claim.statement: Thinking about death makes people less utilitarian
claim.statement_location: Article title and Abstract (title: 'Mortality salience and morality: Thinking about death makes people less utilitarian'; abstract states prediction that individuals under mortality salience would be less likely to give utilitarian responses).

data.source: Human participants recruited on campus at the University of Toulouse (two experiments)
data.wave_or_subset: Two separate experiments (Experiment 1 and Experiment 2) with independent samples
data.sample_size: Experiment 1: 85 participants (59 women; mean age = 22.00, SD = 3.94). Experiment 2: 115 recruited, 5 multivariate outliers removed, final N = 110 (84 women; mean age = 23.77, SD = 5.08).
data.notes: Data consist of binary/percent utilitarian response measures across scenarios and dot-memory task scores (number of correctly localized dots). Experiment 1 used 4 scenarios per participant (two conflict, two control) counterbalanced across 8 questionnaire versions. Experiment 2 included a Dot Memory Task with low (3/3), high (3/4), and very high (4/5) matrices presented for 850 ms (3x3) or 2 s (4x4). Five multivariate outliers were removed in Experiment 2. Scenarios were non-lethal-harm adaptations of Greene et al. (2004); pretest used to select scenarios with conflict version acceptability >= 50%.

method.description: Two laboratory experiments. Mortality salience was manipulated via a standard writing task (thinking/writing about one's own death vs. extreme pain). Participants then judged moral acceptability of actions in moral scenarios presented in conflict (utilitarian response conflicts with intuitive/deontic response) and control (utilitarian response congruent with intuition) versions. Experiment 2 additionally imposed concurrent cognitive load via a Dot Memory Task (low, high, very high load) presented before each moral problem; participants reproduced dot configurations after responding.
steps: Randomly assign participants to mortality salience condition (Death vs Pain). For Experiment 2, additionally assign to cognitive load condition (low, high, very high).
Administer mortality salience manipulation: participants respond in writing to questions about either their own death (Death) or extreme pain (Pain).
Present moral scenarios (two harm-conflict and two harm-control non-lethal scenarios) in counterbalanced order; participants judge moral acceptability of target action.

For Experiment 2: before each moral problem, present a dot-matrix (varying difficulty by load condition) for a brief time; after responding to the moral problem, have participants reproduce the dot configuration to assess load performance.
Record percentage of utilitarian responses per participant per condition; analyze data via ANOVA and t-tests; identify and remove multivariate outliers (Mahalanobis distance) where applicable.
method.models: Analyses included repeated-measures and between-subjects ANOVAs (reporting F statistics and partial eta-squared), independent-samples t-tests and post-hoc t-tests for pairwise comparisons, and Mahalanobis distance for multivariate outlier detection.
method.outcome_variable: Percentage of utilitarian responses (moral acceptability judgments) to conflict and control scenarios; in Experiment 2 also number of correctly localized dots in the Dot Memory Task.
method.independent_variables: Mortality salience condition (Death vs Pain), problem type (conflict vs control), cognitive load level (low, high, very high) in Experiment 2.
method.control_variables: Scenario order counterbalanced across participants; pretest selection of scenarios to avoid floor effects; the Pain condition served as an aversive-emotion control for Death.

results.summary: Experiment 1: Mortality salience (thinking about death) reduced the frequency of utilitarian responses on conflict moral problems but not on control problems. Experiment 2: The mortality salience effect on conflict problems replicated; increasing cognitive load (very high load) also reduced utilitarian responses on conflict problems, and the magnitude of the mortality salience effect was comparable to that of very high cognitive load. No reliable effects on control (no-conflict) problems. Five multivariate outliers were removed in Experiment 2.

numerical_results.value: Death condition produced fewer utilitarian responses on conflict problems; interaction F(1,83)=6.89, p=.01, partial eta-squared = .08; difference on conflict problems t(83)=3.03, p=.003
numerical_results.direction: negative (mortality salience reduced utilitarian responses on conflict problems)

numerical_results.value: ANOVA main effect of mortality salience on conflict problems F(1,110)=8.04, p=.005
numerical_results.unit: statistical test / percentage points reported descriptively (~20 percentage point decrease)

numerical_results.value: ANOVA main effect of cognitive load on conflict problems F(2,110)=6.03, p=.003; very high load produced a significant reduction relative to low (post hoc p=.023) and high (post hoc p=.005) load; drop of ~20 percentage points at very high load
numerical_results.direction: negative (higher cognitive load reduced utilitarian responses)

numerical_results.value: Conflict problems elicited fewer utilitarian responses overall (Experiment 1: F(1,83)=87.46, p<.001, partial eta-squared = .51; Experiment 2: F(1,109)=290.16, p<.001, partial eta-squared = .74)
numerical_results.direction: negative (conflict vs control: fewer utilitarian responses in conflict)

metadata.original_paper_title: Mortality salience and morality: Thinking about death makes people less utilitarian
